FOOSH mechanism — rule out scaphoid fracture; imaging advised.

questions.questionText: Do you experience pain at rest or at night?
questions.question: Do you experience pain at rest or at night?

effects.redFlagText: Rest/night pain — consider Kienbock's disease (AVN of lunate) or advanced CTS.
Rest/night pain — consider Kienbock's disease (AVN of lunate) or advanced CTS.

questions.questionText: Does the pain radiate above the wrist into the forearm or arm?
questions.question: Does the pain radiate above the wrist into the forearm or arm?

effects.notes: Proximal radiation / whole-arm symptoms — consider cervical or proximal nerve origin.
Proximal radiation / whole-arm symptoms — consider cervical or proximal nerve origin.

questions.question: Where are the symptoms felt?

options.value: Thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger

effects.notes: Whole-hand symptoms suggest proximal nerve or systemic cause.

effects.notes: Whole-hand symptoms suggest proximal nerve or systemic cause.

Thenar weakness indicates advanced CTS — consider expedited referral.

questions.question: Does thumb movement or gripping aggravate the pain?

questions.question: Is the pain aggravated by movement?

Severe pain with deformity / inability to move — rule out fracture or dislocation.

questions.question: Does ulnar deviation or rotation of the wrist aggravate the pain?

questions.question: Is the lump hard and immobile, or growing rapidly?

conditions.name: Scaphoid Fracture

questions.questionText: Did the pain begin immediately after a fall?
questions.question: Did the pain begin immediately after a fall?

questions.questionText: Do you still have persistent pain after a fall even if an X-ray was reported normal?
questions.question: Do you still have persistent pain after a fall even if an X-ray was reported normal?

effects.redFlagText: CRITICAL: Persistent snuffbox pain after FOOSH must be treated as a scaphoid fracture despite a normal initial X-ray. Repeat imaging (MRI/CT) required.
CRITICAL: Persistent snuffbox pain after FOOSH must be treated as a scaphoid fracture despite a normal initial X-ray. Repeat imaging (MRI/CT) required.

questions.questionText: Was your presentation delayed (you ignored the injury initially)?
questions.question: Was your presentation delayed (you ignored the injury initially)?

effects.redFlagText: Delayed scaphoid presentation — risk of non-union / AVN; urgent imaging.
Delayed scaphoid presentation — risk of non-union / AVN; urgent imaging.

conditions.clinical_note: Scaphoid fracture is the highest-risk wrist red flag. Persistent snuffbox tenderness after FOOSH must be treated as a fracture and imaged (MRI/CT) even if the initial X-ray is normal, due to non-union/AVN risk.

conditions.name: Kienbock's Disease

questions.questionText: Does wrist loading or activity aggravate the pain?
questions.question: Does wrist loading or activity aggravate the pain?

questions.questionText: Do you have progressive stiffness in the wrist?
questions.question: Do you have progressive stiffness in the wrist?

effects.redFlagText: Progressive stiffness with pain — consistent with Kienbock's (AVN) progression.
Progressive stiffness with pain — consistent with Kienbock's (AVN) progression.

questions.questionText: Is there a history of repetitive trauma or heavy loading of the wrist?
questions.question: Is there a history of repetitive trauma or heavy loading of the wrist?

conditions.clinical_note: No single definitive clinical test exists for Kienbock's disease. Diagnosis is imaging-dependent: X-ray for staging, MRI for early AVN detection.
